title: Chain-of-Agents: End-to-End Agent Foundation Models via Multi-Agent Distillation and Agentic RL
summary: The paper introduces "Chain-of-Agents" (CoA), a novel framework for developing "End-to-End Agent Foundation Models" by dynamically orchestrating a unified model with specialized role-playing and tool agents. It integrates "Multi-Agent Distillation" and "Agentic RL" to achieve this, aiming to create comprehensive, self-contained AI agents. The core idea involves distilling sequential decision-making patterns and agent activation sequences from expert multi-agent systems into 'CoA distillation datasets'. These datasets are then used for Agentic Supervised Fine-tuning, enabling the model to learn adaptive, dynamic multi-agent collaboration within a single inference process, with a focus on computational efficiency evaluation.
key_findings: Proposing "Chain-of-Agents" as a novel architectural paradigm for agent development.
Leveraging "Multi-Agent Distillation" as a key technique for training agent foundation models.
Employing "Agentic RL" as a specialized reinforcement learning mechanism.
Focusing on the "End-to-End" development of agent foundation models.
methodology: The Chain-of-Agents (CoA) framework orchestrates a unified model (Mθ) with specialized role-playing (e.g., Thinking, Plan, Reflection) and tool agents (e.g., Search, Crawl, Code Generate) to tackle complex queries. Its core technical approach is multi-agent knowledge distillation, which extracts complete execution trajectories (reasoning states, activated agents, observations) from expert multi-agent systems like OAgents. These extracted trajectories form 'CoA distillation datasets' used for Agentic Supervised Fine-tuning, allowing the model to learn dynamic agent orchestration via state transitions, with progressive quality filtering applied to exclude overly simplistic tasks.

data.summary: The paper introduces "Chain-of-Agents," a framework aimed at developing "End-to-End Agent Foundation Models." It proposes to achieve this by integrating two primary methodologies: "Multi-Agent Distillation" and "Agentic RL." The core objective appears to be the creation of comprehensive, self-contained AI agents through a structured multi-agent approach combined with advanced learning techniques.
key_contributions: Proposing "Chain-of-Agents" as a novel architectural paradigm for agent development.
Leveraging "Multi-Agent Distillation" as a key technique for training agent foundation models.
Employing "Agentic RL" as a specialized reinforcement learning mechanism.
Focusing on the "End-to-End" development of agent foundation models.
data.novelty_assessment: The novelty appears to stem from the specific integration and orchestration of several advanced concepts. While multi-agent systems, distillation, and reinforcement learning are established, their combination under the "Chain-of-Agents" paradigm for building "End-to-End Agent Foundation Models," particularly with the explicit mention of "Agentic RL," suggests a genuinely new architectural and training methodology. The approach aims to create comprehensive AI agents by chaining specialized agents and distilling their knowledge, which could represent a significant step beyond incremental improvements in individual agent components.

data.methodology_detail: The Chain-of-Agents (CoA) framework tackles complex queries by dynamically orchestrating a unified model Mθ with specialized role-playing (e.g., Thinking, Plan, Reflection) and tool agents (e.g., Search, Crawl, Code Generate). Its methodology involves multi-agent knowledge distillation, where sequential decision-making patterns and agent activation sequences from state-of-the-art multi-agent systems are extracted to generate training data. This data is then used for Agentic Supervised Fine-tuning, allowing the model to learn adaptive, dynamic multi-agent collaboration within a single inference process.
data.technical_approach: The core technical approach is multi-agent knowledge distillation, which extends sequence-level knowledge distillation to capture complete execution trajectories of expert multi-agent systems. Specifically, the OAgents system is used as a teacher to extract trajectories, formalized as sequences of reasoning states, activated agents, and observations (τ = {(St,ϕt,ot)}T t=1). These extracted trajectories form 'CoA distillation datasets' which are then used for Agentic Supervised Fine-tuning of the Chain-of-Agents model, enabling it to learn dynamic agent orchestration via state transitions (St = fθ(St−1,ϕt−1,ot−1)). A progressive quality filtering mechanism is applied to these trajectories, excluding overly simplistic tasks.
datasets_used: CoA distillation datasets (generated from OAgents trajectories)

key_findings: The paper outlines a methodology for evaluating computational efficiency, comparing tool calls and token consumption across OAgents, WebThinker, and AFM.
The computational efficiency evaluation uses 10 randomly sampled instances from the GAIA dataset.
The paper indicates that code generation benchmarks will be reported using the Pass@1 metric.
